Case report

## Unexpected finding of encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis: mind the cocoon

Eva van Boven,  Robert R J Coebergh van den Braak,  Jan van Laar, 1 2 3 Michiel G H Betjes 4

## SUMMARY

Here, we present a fatal case of a man in his 40s with encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis (EPS). In retrospect, a spot diagnosis on the abdominal CT scan. The patient presented with progressive abdominal complaints of pain and vomiting over the last 2 months. He had a history of therapy-refractory sarcoidosis. A CT scan was performed, and loculated ascites was noted. The patient was admitted with suspected spontaneous peritoneal peritonitis but did not improve on antibiotics. Data from the microbiology, pathology and radiology investigations lead to no diagnosis. Eventually, laparoscopy revealed the diagnosis of EPS. Treatment with tamoxifen to counteract fibrosis and a pulse dose of methylprednisolone was started. Unfortunately, the patient developed a bowel perforation and died. In retrospect, the radiological sign of a cocoon encasing the small bowels should have been a clue to an early diagnosis.

## BACKGROUND

Encapsulating  peritoneal  sclerosis  (EPS)  is  a  rare condition of inflammation of the peritoneum leading  to  fibrosis  and  eventually  encasement  of bowel loops by a fibrotic cocoon. This can result in bowel obstruction and possible perforation. It is associated with high morbidity and 1-year mortality after  diagnosis  reaches  42-50%. 1 2 A  diagnosis  of EPS is mostly made in patients on or shortly after stopping peritoneal dialysis. The long-term continuous exposure to peritoneal dialysis fluid, causing low-grade  inflammation  in  the  peritoneum,  may eventually lead to exaggerated fibrosis with EPS in a  minority  of  patients.   Infrequently,  other  causes 3 such as systemic disease, infection or medication are associated with the aetiology of EPS. 4

The  classic  presentation  of  EPS  is  insidious. Patients present with abdominal pain (86%), nausea and vomiting (54%) and fever (29%). Half of the patients are diagnosed by radiographic findings, while the other half are diagnosed by surgical findings. 5

## CASE PRESENTATION

A man in his 40s presented to the emergency department  with  slowly  progressive  abdominal  pain  for several months recently accompanied by vomiting.

He has a history  of  type  1  diabetes  mellitus  in the  last  13  years  complicated  by  kidney  failure, resulting in haemodialysis. Extrapulmonary sarcoidosis was diagnosed in the last 8 years with biopsy-proven skin and liver involvement leading to

Figure 1 CT scan of the abdomen: cocoon encasing small bowels. Arrow 1 showing ascites and arrow 2 showing encapsulating peritoneal membrane.

<!-- image -->

portal  hypertension.  The  treatment  of  sarcoidosis is  plagued  by  side  effects  including  intolerance  to different  medication,  methotrexate-induced  bone marrow  toxicity  and  antibody  formation  against infliximab.  At  the  moment  of  presentation,  the treatment  consists  of  adalimumab  for  1  year  in combination  with  methylprednisolone  1g  once  a month, followed by tapering. The methylprednisolone was discontinued 6 months before presentation due to another side effect: osteonecrosis of the hallux as a result of cumulative steroid exposure.

The patient reported progressive abdominal pain for several months located in the epigastrium. The pain worsened with eating, and he lost 6 kg in  weight.  Lately,  the  duration  of  the  pain  was increased and accompanied by vomiting. On examination, the patient was evidently painful. He was haemodynamic stable and had no fever (blood pressure 144/90mm Hg, pulse rate 86 beats per minute, body  temperature  36.5°C).  Physical  examination showed  abdomen  distension  on  inspection  with lively  bowel  sounds  on  auscultation,  diffuse  dullness on percussion, painful palpation with guarding and diffuse rebound tenderness.

## INVESTIGATIONS

Laboratory results showed C reactive protein 63mg/L, leucocyte counts 9.4×10 6 /L, lipase 32U/L,  slightly  elevated  alkalic  phosphatase  and gamma-GT values without elevated transaminases.

van Boven E, et al . BMJ Case Rep 2025; 18 :e264334. doi:10.1136/bcr-2024-264334

Case report

Figure 2 CT scan of the abdomen: cocoon encasing small bowels. Arrow 1 showing ascites and arrow 2 showing encapsulating peritoneal membrane.

<!-- image -->

A CT scan of the abdomen showed ascites, mostly in the upper abdomen, which appeared to be loculated. An abdominal punction with aspiration of ascites fluid was performed to investigate the possibility of bacterial peritonitis. Abdominal fluid revealed elevated  counts  of  neutrophils  (0.9×10 /L)  and  erythrocyte 9 count (1.7×10 12 /L). The latter was considered to be the result of a traumatic punction. The patient was treated with ceftriaxone intravenously for assumed spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. The clinical condition deteriorated over the next few days. Repeated CT scans  showed  thickening  and  enhancement  of  the  peritoneum and ascites (figures 1 and 2).

The  ascites  fluid  tested  negative  for  tuberculosis  and  nontuberculosis mycobacterium by auramine stain, PCR and culture and cultures for common and uncommon pathogens. The pathologist found no indication of malignant cells in the ascites. No monoclonal cell population was found by flow cytometry. The

Figure 3 Laparotomy revealing fibrosis around small bowels forming a sclerotic cocoon.

<!-- image -->

2

van Boven E, et al . BMJ Case Rep 2025; 18 :e264334. doi:10.1136/bcr-2024-264334

soluble IL-  2R was high at 4844  U/L (ULN<555), with previous levels  around  2000  U/L.  To  test  for  peritoneal  localisation  of sarcoidosis, a surgical peritoneal biopsy by laparoscopy was set up. During the laparoscopy (later confirmed by laparotomy), a tight, thickened visceral peritoneum was seen encasing the small bowels, as a cocoon, consistent with EPS. A biopsy of the sclerotic membrane was not performed as the risk for bowel perforation was considered to be high (see figure 3).

## DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

EPS is a rare disease in patients not on peritoneal dialysis, and the  initial presentation  is  non-  specific with  gastrointestinal complaints and abdominal pain. In this case, the CT scan already showed a thickened  peritoneum  encasing  the  small  intestines, giving  away  the  diagnosis  of  EPS.  The  presence  of  a  sclerotic cocoon was confirmed by laparotomy.

The  patient  presented  with  abdominal  pain,  vomiting  and bloody  ascites.  Initially,  spontaneous  bacterial  peritonitis  was suspected, and the bloody ascites was explained by a traumatic paracentesis since these two diagnoses are most common. But the patient did not improve after 1  week of adequate antibiotic therapy, and the cultures did not show growth of bacteria. The differential  diagnosis  of  bloody  ascites  is  diverse  and  involves infectious  causes  (mostly  tuberculosis  of  non-  mycobacterium tuberculosis),  varices  bleeding  intraabdominal  (given  that  the patient  had  a  history  of  portal  hypertension),  malignancy  (eg, hepatic, haematological, any pelvic organ, peritoneal mesothelioma, etc) and inflammation of the peritoneum due to systemic disease. The latter is of interest as the patient was known with a history of severe and refractory sarcoidosis, and case reports of EPS caused by autoimmune disease and in particularly sarcoidosis are described. 6 7

Since  thorough  investigations  for  this  differential  diagnosis were inconclusive, a laparoscopy was performed. The diagnosis of EPS was established by the typical macroscopical appearance of  encasement  of  the  bowels  within  a  thick  fibrous  cocoon. Figures 1 and 2 are taken during a later laparotomy. No visceral peritoneal biopsies were taken for fear of bowel perforation.

The differential diagnosis of the aetiology of the EPS is broadly the same as the differential diagnosis of bloody ascites, except for drug-  induced causes. 4 8 The previous medications were evaluated for a potential trigger, but no potential EPS-  inducing drug could be identified. EPS caused by sarcoidosis disease activity in the peritoneum could not be proven as histological examination of the peritoneum was not performed. The levels of soluble IL2R were high, even for a patient on dialysis, but are not specific for disease activity of sarcoidosis, since it can also be elevated due to EPS. 1 9

## TREATMENT

The  patient  was  treated  with  high-  dose  methylprednisolone (3  days 1000  mg) despite the relative contraindication of osteonecrosis of the hallux and tamoxifen 20  mg two times per day as antifibrosis treatment. Total parenteral nutrition was started in combination with laxative treatment.

## OUTCOME AND FOLLOW-UP

Unfortunately,  the  patient  developed  bowel  perforation  with abdominal  sepsis  unresponsive  to  broad-  spectrum  antibiotics. Haemodynamic  support  was  required. A laparotomy was performed, again showing EPS with signs of bowel perforation but without identification of the site of perforation and surgical options. A peel surgery with enterolysis was considered, but the

clinical condition was insufficient for such a high-  risk intervention.  He  died  3  weeks  after  treatment  was  started  because  of refractory septic shock.

## DISCUSSION

EPS is a rare disease and mostly seen in relation to peritoneal dialysis treatment. Since it has a non-  specific presentation and is potentially fatal if treatment is delayed, it is important to recognise it timely.

There is no golden standard for the diagnosis of EPS. Bloody ascites  and elevated C reactive protein can be seen as a result of the inflamed peritoneum.  The typical radiological descrip4 tion  of  EPS  is  loculated  ascites  (48%),  thickened  peritoneum (30%), peritoneum calcifications (28%), small bowel tethering (22%) and dilated bowel loops (9%). 2 4 The typical laparoscopic image is a thickened, brownish peritoneum, cocoon-  like bowel encapsulation,  adherent  loops  of  small  bowel  and  peritoneal calcification.  The bloody ascites is thought to be the result of 4 increased  angioneogenesis. 10  11 Bowel  perforation  is  a  feared complication. 12

The  general  hypothesis  on  pathogenesis  is  that  a  certain trigger can activate the process of fibrosis. The consensus is that this  is  a  multifactorial  process,  which  occurs  at  a  certain  time due to a potential trigger. 12 The cascade results in inflammation, angiogenesis stimulated by cytokines and growth factors leading to myofibroblast proliferation and matrix accumulation, eventually resulting in fibrosis. 13 In this case, the trigger was not fully understood. It can be hypothesised that in this case, EPS resulted from  sarcoidosis  activity  at  the  level  of  the  peritoneum  since peritoneal  involvement  of  sarcoidosis  is  described. 14-18 There is one case report of EPS due to sarcoidosis.  Especially in this 6 patient with severe refractory sarcoidosis in whom the sarcoidosis treatment was de-  escalated a few months prior, sarcoidosis was  considered  a  possible  cause  for  the  EPS.  The  high  elevations of s-  IL2R levels are not conclusive for sarcoidosis activity, as they may result of kidney failure, especially in combination with active EPS.  Unfortunately, in this case no diagnostic biopsy 9 could  safely  be  acquired  because  of  the  risk  of  perforation. Therefore,  a  definitive  histological  diagnosis  of  sarcoidosisrelated EPS could not be made.

First,  the  treatment  of  EPS  should  be  directed  at  removing the  trigger  or  treating  the  underlying  condition.  If  there  is no  underlying  condition  or  the  patient  deteriorates  despite removing the trigger, treatment with glucocorticosteroids (GC) or tamoxifen is indicated. It is key to start treatment early in the process to prevent progression of inflammation to the formation of fibrosis, but only after infectious causes have been excluded. The treatment guidelines are not based on high-  level evidence but are mostly based on clinical experience with EPS after peritoneal dialysis, given the fact that other causes of EPS are very rare. GC are widely used for the treatment of EPS; an early case series  reports  100%  mortality  without  GC  and  survival  of  at least over 1 year for all the cases treated with GC between 1997 and 2000. 19 Other case series have been presented since then, indicating improvement of EPS from GC in part of the patients, but  the  effect  is  dependent  on  the  timing  of  the  treatment  in the course of EPS. The proposed dose is pulse therapy of methylprednisolone 1000  mg for 3  days followed by 1  mg/kg/day for 1  month, and then tapered slowly in a year. 20

An alternative or additional treatment for EPS is tamoxifen. The suggestion for tamoxifen comes from the analogy of retroperitoneal fibrosis. The mode of action is suppressing the fibroblasts  by  blocking  transforming  growth  factorβ 1  signalling.

van Boven E, et al . BMJ Case Rep 2025; 18 :e264334. doi:10.1136/bcr-2024-264334

Case report

The effect of tamoxifen is analysed in a retrospective study of 63 patients of whom 24 patients were treated with tamoxifen. There was a trend towards improved survival in the tamoxifentreated group. 1

Advised supportive care in the process is to start early with feeding enteric or parental. Attention should be given to laxatives unless there is a complete obstruction.

When medical treatment fails, there is an option for surgical treatment with decortication and enterolysis. Patients should be referred to a specialised referral centre since it is a low-  volume, very complex operation with a high mortality risk, ranging from 19 to 34.5%. 21-23 Even after successful surgery, there is a risk of recurrence of 10-20%. 22 24 25

## Learning points

- Ź Mind the cocoon. In hindsight, the CT images show a typical 'football' shaped cocoon in which the bowels were encapsulated by a thickened peritoneum.
- Ź Early recognition of encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis is key to prevent fibrosis.
- Ź Patients can best be treated in an expert centre and when the patient's condition deteriorates, a surgical expert centre ought to be consulted.
- Ź We can only hope to make the vignette more known to physicians for the benefit of the very few patients who unfortunately develop this mutilating fibrosis of the peritoneum since early treatment is key.

Contributors All authors (EvB, RRJCvdB, JvL and MGHB) contributed significantly in the writing, data collection and analysis. MGHB is the guarantor.

Funding The authors have not declared a specific grant for this research from any funding agency in the public, commercial or not-  for-  profit sectors.

Competing interests None declared.

Provenance and peer review Not commissioned; externally peer reviewed.

Case reports provide a valuable learning resource for the scientific community and can indicate areas of interest for future research. They should not be used in isolation to guide treatment choices or public health policy.
